You can have the correct paper to support your claim, use a system for bibtex that does not produce accurate outputs, and then get the wrong bibtex. Calling the reference *as a whole* a hallucination further overstretches what the term “hallucination” means. Instead, it’s an

Food for peer review thought: A citation isn’t “hallucinated” if it’s *grounded* on an actual paper. It may include hallucinated or incorrect *components* — eg, one author is wrong or the DOI is off by 1 — but the citation as a whole is not a “hallucination”.
